fuzzy_prd:
Our authorization server needs a shared set of small, pure helper functions for deciding whether a request is permitted. These cover several concerns: (1) scope matching — given the set of scopes a client was actually granted, decide whether a requested scope is covered, with support for three matching strategies: one that treats scopes as a dot-separated hierarchy where broader scopes cover narrower ones; one that supports wildcard segments; and one that requires an exact match; (2) set and sequence comparisons — unordered case-insensitive equality, ordered case-sensitive equality, contains-all, contains-any, single-value equality, and space-joined equality for comparing lists of permission arguments; (3) two low-level string helpers: case-insensitive membership check and a minimal escaper for embedding strings safely in JSON; (4) audience checks — URI-aware matching (same scheme, host, and path prefix) and exact-string matching; and (5) redirect-URI validation — loopback/localhost detection, transport-security checks in standard and strict modes, and matching a requested URI against a registered set. Additionally we need a utility to append a key=value pair to the fragment component of a URL. All functions are pure, take plain inputs, and return plain outputs — no I/O or global state. Follow the same loopback-detection logic as the existing token-endpoint host check.

A couple follow-ups from the team’s questions. On wildcard scope matching, if the pattern has fewer segments than the request, its FINAL segment must be '*' to match the extra segments. A non-'*' final segment only matches when the pattern and request have the same segment count. Also, an empty request segment is never matched by '*'.

On unordered case-insensitive set equality in Matches, probe items that collapse to the same case-folded value effectively reduce the set size below the reference length, causing a length mismatch and returning false. Example: reference=['foo','foo'], probe=['foo','foo'] returns false (both probe items are the same case-folded value).

Also, for the localhost / loopback piece, a URL is treated as localhost when its host is exactly 'localhost', '127.0.0.1', or '::1', OR when the host ends with the suffix '.localhost'. Port is ignored for this check.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature:
func HierarchicScopeStrategy(scopes fosite.Arguments, scope string) bool
func WildcardScopeStrategy(scopes fosite.Arguments, scope string) bool
func ExactScopeStrategy(scopes fosite.Arguments, scope string) bool
func (a Arguments) Matches(items ...string) bool
func (a Arguments) MatchesExact(items ...string) bool
func (a Arguments) HasOneOf(items ...string) bool
func (a Arguments) Has(items ...string) bool
func (a Arguments) ExactOne(item string) bool
func (a Arguments) Equals(item string) bool
func IsLocalhost(redirectURL *url.URL) bool
func IsSecureRedirectURI(redirectURL *url.URL) bool
func IsRedirectURISecureStrict(redirectURL *url.URL) bool

trigger_keywords: secure redirect URI rule with http
when is http scheme considered secure for redirects
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Standard security (IsSecureRedirectURI): 'https' is always secure; 'http' is secure only when the host is localhost/loopback. Strict security (IsRedirectURISecureStrict): only 'https' or 'http' to localhost; custom schemes are NOT accepted.

trigger_keywords: when does URI audience match with different paths
path prefix check in audience matching
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: URI-aware audience matching: same scheme and host, and requested path must either equal the whitelisted path exactly or start with the whitelisted path followed by '/'. Exact string audience matching: byte-for-byte identity, no URL parsing.

trigger_keywords: how is key=value appended to URL fragment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The fragment encoding utility writes 'key=value' into the URL fragment component, replacing any existing fragment. The full URL with the new fragment is the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same loopback-detection logic as the existing token-endpoint host check
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The IsLocalhost function in authorize_helper.go / helper.go, which checks for 'localhost', '127.0.0.1', '::1', and the '.localhost' suffix.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Consult scope_strategy.go, arguments.go, audience_strategy.go, and authorize_helper.go in the golden source directory. The dispatcher at rcb_tests/dispatcher/main.go shows the op-to-function routing.

feature1_1_hierarchic_scope.json.description: Hierarchic scope matching. A request scope is granted when one of the granted scopes either equals it exactly, or is a dot-separated prefix of it (a broader scope such as 'a.b' covers any more specific 'a.b.c'); a granted scope can never satisfy a request that is broader than itself. The input names the matching strategy, a set of granted scopes, and a list of requested scopes; for each request the output reports whether it is allowed.

cases.expected_output:
request=foo.bar.baz allowed=true
request=baz.baz.baz allowed=true
request=foo.bar allowed=true
request=foo allowed=false
request=bar.baz allowed=true
request=bar.baz.zad allowed=true
request=bar allowed=false
request=baz allowed=false

feature1_2_wildcard_scope.json.description: Wildcard scope matching. Scopes are split on dots into segments; a granted pattern matches a request when it has no more segments than the request and every segment matches positionally, where a '*' segment matches exactly one non-empty request segment. A trailing pattern segment that is not '*' only matches when the segment counts are equal. The input names the strategy, the granted patterns, and the requested scopes; for each request the output reports whether it is allowed.

cases.expected_output:
request=foo.bar allowed=true
request=foo.baz allowed=true
request=foo.bar.baz allowed=true
request=foo allowed=false

feature1_3_exact_scope.json.description: Exact scope matching. A request scope is granted only when it is byte-for-byte identical to one of the granted scopes; no prefix or wildcard semantics apply. The input names the strategy, the granted scopes, and the requested scopes; for each request the output reports whether it is allowed.

cases.expected_output:
request=foo.bar.baz allowed=true
request=foo.bar allowed=true
request=foo.bar.baz.baz allowed=false
request=foo.bar.bar allowed=false
request=foo.bar.baz1 allowed=false
request=foo.bar1 allowed=false

feature2_1_match_unordered.json.description: Unordered, case-insensitive set equality between a reference list and a probe list. The two lists match only when they have the same length and every probe item is present in the reference list ignoring letter case and ignoring order. Because membership is case-insensitive, repeated items that collapse to the same value cause a length mismatch and therefore do NOT match. The input is a list of checks, each with the reference 'arguments' and the probe 'items'; for each check the output reports whether they match.

cases.expected_output:
matched=true
matched=true
matched=true
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=true
matched=true
matched=false
matched=false

feature2_2_match_ordered.json.description: Ordered, case-sensitive sequence equality between a reference list and a probe list. The two lists match only when they have the same length and, at every position, the items are byte-for-byte identical. Order and letter case both matter, but exact duplicates in the same positions are allowed. The input is a list of checks, each with the reference 'arguments' and the probe 'items'; for each check the output reports whether they match.

cases.expected_output:
matched=true
matched=true
matched=true
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=true

feature2_3_contains_all.json.description: Case-insensitive 'contains all' check: every probe item must be present somewhere in the reference list, compared ignoring letter case and ignoring order. The reference list may contain extra items not probed. The input is a list of checks, each with the reference 'arguments' and the probe 'items'; for each check the output reports whether all probes are present.

cases.expected_output:
has_all=true
has_all=true
has_all=true
has_all=false
has_all=true
has_all=true
has_all=false
has_all=false

feature2_4_contains_any.json.description: Case-insensitive 'contains any' check: the result is true when at least one probe item is present in the reference list, compared ignoring letter case. The input is a list of checks, each with the reference 'arguments' and the probe 'items'; for each check the output reports whether any probe is present.

feature2_5_single_value.json.description: Single-value equality: the result is true only when the reference list holds exactly one item and that item is byte-for-byte identical to the probe value. A list with zero or multiple items never matches, and a multi-word probe is never split. The input is a list of checks, each with the reference 'arguments' and a single probe 'value'; for each check the output reports whether it matches.

cases.expected_output:
matched=true
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false

feature2_6_space_joined.json.description: Space-joined equality: the reference list is joined into a single string using one space between items, and the result is true when that joined string is byte-for-byte identical to the probe value. Thus a probe like 'foo bar' matches a two-item list of 'foo' and 'bar'. The input is a list of checks, each with the reference 'arguments' and a single probe 'value'; for each check the output reports whether it matches.

cases.expected_output:
matched=true
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=false
matched=true

feature3_1_membership.json.description: Case-insensitive membership test: report whether a needle string is present in a haystack list, comparing ignoring letter case. The input is a list of checks, each with a 'needle' and a 'haystack'; for each check the output reports whether the needle is present.

cases.expected_output:
present=true
present=true
present=false
present=false
present=true
present=false

feature3_2_json_escape.json.description: Minimal JSON string escaping: produce a string safe to place inside JSON double quotes by escaping the backslash, the double-quote character, and every control character (code points U+0000 through U+001F) as a six-character \u00xx sequence. Ordinary characters, including printable punctuation, pass through unchanged. The input supplies the raw 'value'; the output is the escaped form on its own line.

feature4_1_audience_uri.json.description: URI-aware audience matching. Each requested audience is allowed only when some whitelisted audience has the same scheme and host and a path that either equals the requested path or is a prefix of it at a path-segment boundary (a trailing slash on either side is ignored). An empty request list is always allowed. Strings that are not URLs are compared by these same URL rules (empty scheme/host), so plain tokens match only an identical whitelist entry. The input is a list of checks, each with a 'whitelist' and the 'requested' audiences; for each check the output reports whether all requested audiences are allowed.

cases.expected_output:
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=true
allowed=true

feature4_2_audience_exact.json.description: Exact-string audience matching. Each requested audience is allowed only when it is byte-for-byte identical to some whitelisted audience; no URL parsing or trailing-slash tolerance applies. An empty request list is always allowed. The input is a list of checks, each with a 'whitelist' and the 'requested' audiences; for each check the output reports whether all requested audiences are allowed.

cases.expected_output:
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=true
allowed=true
allowed=false
allowed=false
allowed=false

feature5_1_localhost.json.description: Loopback / localhost host detection for a redirect URI. A URL is treated as localhost when its host name is exactly 'localhost', '127.0.0.1', or '::1', or when it ends with the suffix '.localhost'; an optional port does not change the result. The input is a list of URL strings; for each URL the output reports whether it is a localhost address.

cases.expected_output:
localhost=false
localhost=true
localhost=true
localhost=true
localhost=true
localhost=true
localhost=true

feature5_2_secure.json.description: Transport-security check for a redirect URI. A URL is considered secure unless it uses the 'http' scheme with a non-localhost host; 'https' is always secure, plain 'http' is secure only for localhost addresses, and non-http schemes (custom app schemes) are considered secure. The input is a list of URL strings; for each URL the output reports whether it is secure.

cases.expected_output:
secure=false
secure=true
secure=true
secure=true
secure=true
secure=true
secure=true
secure=true
secure=false
secure=true

feature5_3_secure_strict.json.description: Strict transport-security check for a redirect URI. Only 'https', or 'http' pointing at a localhost address, is accepted; every other scheme, including custom application schemes, is rejected. The input is a list of URL strings; for each URL the output reports whether it passes the strict check.

cases.expected_output:
strict_secure=false
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=true
strict_secure=false
strict_secure=false

feature5_4_redirect_match.json.description: Match a requested redirect URI against a client's pre-registered redirect URIs. If the requested URI is empty and the client registered exactly one valid redirect URI, that registered URI is used. Otherwise the requested URI must match a registered one by simple string comparison, with one exception: for loopback addresses (http with host 127.0.0.1 or [::1]) the port is ignored, so a registered loopback URI matches the same path/query on any port. A matched URI must itself be a valid absolute redirect URI (absolute, no fragment). On success the resolved redirect URI is returned; otherwise a neutral mismatch error is reported. The input is a list of checks, each with the client's 'registered' URIs and the 'request' URI; for each check the output reports the resolved URI or an error.

cases.expected_output:
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=wta://auth
redirect_uri=wta:///auth
redirect_uri=wta://foo/auth
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=https://bar.com/cb
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=https://bar.com/cb
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=http://[::1]:1024
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=http://[::1]:1024/cb
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=http://127.0.0.1:1024
redirect_uri=http://127.0.0.1:64000/cb
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=http://127.0.0.1
redirect_uri=http://127.0.0.1:8080/Cb
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=http://127.0.0.1:8080/cb?foo=bar
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
error=redirect_uri_mismatch
redirect_uri=web+application://callback
redirect_uri=https://google.com/?foo=bar%20foo+baz

feature6_fragment_encode.json.description: Encode a single parameter (key with one value) into the fragment component of a URL. The given key and value are written as 'key=value' into the URL fragment, replacing any existing fragment and preserving the rest of the URL (scheme, host, query). When the resulting URL is serialized, characters in the fragment that require percent-encoding (such as a space) are encoded. The input supplies the base 'url' and a 'fragment' map of one key to a single-element value list; the output is the serialized URL including the new fragment.
